title: Pizzelle
subtitle: Thin Italian wafer biscuits embossed with a snowflake pattern
excerpt: Crisp Italian biscuits cooked on a decorative iron, embossed with a snowflake or geometric pattern, flavoured with vanilla or anise. Eaten flat, rolled into cones, or curved over a mould.
sourceNotes: Pizzelle are one of the oldest Italian biscuits, documented in the Abruzzo region of central Italy from at least the fifteenth century. The embossed iron used to cook them is passed down in Italian families as an heirloom. The name derives from the Italian word for flat and round. Traditional flavourings are anise or vanilla; lemon and almond versions are also well established.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Pizzelle keep in an airtight tin for up to one week and improve in crispness after the first day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: cooked on a pizzelle iron or thin waffle iron at medium-high heat; no oven required

glossaryTerms.definition: A very thin, crisp biscuit cooked between two heated plates or irons. The batter is more liquid than most biscuit doughs and sets into its shape as it cooks.

content.text: biscuit from the Abruzzo region, one of the oldest in Italian baking. They are made with a light batter poured onto a specially embossed iron, which imprints a snowflake or geometric pattern onto the surface as they cook. The iron is the essential piece of equipment: a standard Belgian waffle iron makes a functional but undecorated equivalent.

The flavour is traditionally vanilla or anise. This recipe uses vanilla; a teaspoon of almond extract gives a pleasant alternative. Pizzelle can be eaten flat, or shaped while still warm: rolled around a dowel for cannoli-style tubes, or curved over a bowl for ice-cream baskets. Once cool, they are rigid.

Whisk {{eggs}} and {{caster-sugar}} together until pale and slightly thickened. Add {{unsalted-butter}} and {{vanilla-extract}} and whisk to combine. Sift {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} over the wet ingredients and fold until a smooth, thick batter forms — similar to a thick pancake batter.

Heat the pizzelle iron to medium-high. Lightly brush both plates with neutral oil. Drop a heaped teaspoon of batter onto the centre of each mould. Close the iron and cook for 45–60 seconds until the steam slows and the wafers are golden — a deep golden brown at the edges and pale gold at the centre. Open carefully; the wafers are fragile when hot. Lift out with a palette knife and lay flat on a cooling rack. They crisp up within 30 seconds.

To make tuile cones: immediately on leaving the iron, roll each wafer around a cone mould or a thick pencil. Hold for 10 seconds until set. To make cups: drape over an upturned small bowl or ramekin and press gently. Both shapes are formed in the 20-second window before the wafer becomes brittle.
The batter thickens as it stands. Add a tablespoon of warm water if it becomes too stiff to spread in the iron after the first few batches.
For anise flavour: replace the vanilla with 1 teaspoon of almond extract and 1 teaspoon of anise extract, or use 1 tablespoon of Sambuca.
A thin Belgian waffle iron works but gives a different embossed pattern and slightly thicker texture.
